title: 2026/03/12 EDSI
displayTitle: 2026-03-11 - Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee - Commerce (Promoting Competition and Other Matters) Amendment Bill
committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
topic: Commerce (Promoting Competition and Other Matters) Amendment Bill
description: Thursday 12 March 2026 | Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee Commerce (Promoting Competition and Other Matters) Amendment Bill Economics NZ Donal Curtin, Managing Director DLA Piper Mark Williamson, Partner Kannu Sachdev, Solicitor The Banking Reform Coalition Kent Duston, Convenor Patrick Duignan
descriptionSummary: Commerce (Promoting Competition and Other Matters) Amendment Bill. Economics NZ. Donal Curtin, Managing Director

chunks.text: Good morning. Can you hear me? I can indeed. Good morning. Oh, good. Thank you so much. Thank you for your submission. Uh, we have 10 minutes with you have been speaking to your submission. If there is any time left, there might be questions from members, if that's okay with you. That, that's lovely. Madam Chair, uh, committee members, and thank you for the opportunity to submit In, in the time I've got, I think I'll just concentrate on two of the points I made.

Uh, one of them is about market studies. Uh, the commission, as you know, already has the power to do market studies and to things like, uh, petrol stations, supermarkets, what have you. And the minister responsible is required to respond to that market study within a reasonable time.

Now, the, the bill, as it stands, promotes a new power for the commission to do a different kind of market study, which is a regulation study. And, uh, so that they can look into things like whether, you know, land planning is preventing supermarkets opening at the moment, as the bill stands.

Um, the minister is not required to respond in the way that is required to respond to a normal market study. So my submission is that exactly the same requirements should be put on the minister to respond to a commission regulation study as is currently, or she is required to do, uh, to the regular market studies.

And I think this would be a very good accountability mechanism because then you can see openly what ideas the minister has decided to run with or not run with, and you can see, and also whether the ideas of the commissioner worked out. So I think it's really important that there is an accountability mechanism in there for ministers to respond to regulation, uh, market studies and, and give you folks in particular a chance to pick over exactly what happened and make sure that the money that the commissioner has spent on these studies

isn't just ignored in a ministerial office somewhere. So that, that was my first point. Uh, the second one, and I'm sure you've had a lot on this already, is about predatory pricing. And my advice to you was, if, if it's in doubt, don't do it. Um, and I've got a couple of reasons for that.

Uh, one of them is that we can all think very easily of products that are actually given away for free. And there's no question of the people supplying them, uh, trying to recover the cost. They're not predatory pricing. Uh, nobody around the table, for example, has ever paid for a software browser.

You get it free. Chrome came free, Firefox came free. Uh, none of you has ever paid for a community newspaper. Uh, none of you has paid for any TV NZ programs. And there are reasons for that. In all these cases, people are giving away the product well below cost.

It can't be further below cost of free. And, uh, because they're in the business of trying to get, uh, as many eyeballs as possible in front of their advertisers. And it seems to me that there's a risk, uh, that predatory pricing introduced, uh, as it stands risk cutting across something that is not only commercially sensible, but it's actually valuable to consumers.

Uh, I can think of other examples beyond Sky tv, uh, being, uh, having to face, uh, free TV NZ programs. Uh, there are plenty of them around. So my advice would be to think long and hard about going down the bread free pricing route. And my very, very final comment, leaving a little bit of time for questions, is on p pricing.

The Aussies did exactly what's proposed in the bill, uh, that for us a few years back and junked it, and they decided, look, we'll give a go, have a go at writing in p pricing into our competition law. It's not working and backed away, and the IES don't get everything right.

But I think on this occasion, they, they might have, might have the right end of the stick. So that's very much what I wanted to say. There are other points I made, but you've all had a chance to read them anyway, and very much, uh, happy to take any questions. Thank you. Uh, there is a question from Dr. David Wilson. Am I right in saying Cher to you, Donald? It looks like she's You're indeed. Good, good.

Fol Roth. Thank you. Um, and I know you're sitting in the Trinity Library there, that's, uh, Well spotted. Um, I want to pick up on, yes, we, I think we all sort of agree with you on some of your comments around predatory pricing. It's a difficult topic, but I wanna switch to, um, where you're saying that the rights of the commission, um, to investigate anti-competitive behavior, you think this is an overly, uh, it's overly intrusive into the activities of third parties.

In other words, we're getting all of this information for free by legislating towards that end. Yeah. However, we've had others that have said this is gonna really give some great insight into the, in those firms. So can you just explain to me why you think it's overly intrusive? Yeah, I did. I, I frankly, I thought, uh, the, the requirement that the proposed requirement that the commission should be able to go and make firms do calculations according to the commission's methodology seemed to me a little bit of a cheek, frankly.

Um, the commission already has compulsory data gathering powers, uh, it can get any data in it likes, uh, under existing legislature. I think the section 98 orders don't, don't hold me to that, but I think that's what they are. So, again, gather, uh, any information, it likes board minutes, you know, emails, yada, yada.

Uh, so I don't think it's at all confined in its ability to gather information. I think that's just fine. Uh, my point was I thought they were pushing the boat out a little bit too far in requiring third parties to do complicated calculations on the commission's behalf. I mean, what is the commission for? That's its job.
Right? Thank you. Thank you so much, Donald, for your submission. Thank you. You're welcome. Bye-bye. Um, next, uh, if we have DLA pipe. Really? Yeah. Correct. Thank you.

Good Morning. Good morning is good morning. Correct. Uh, Thank you so much for your submission. We have 10 minutes with you, right? So after you have finished, uh, speaking to your submission, if there is any time left, there might be questions from members, if that's okay with you. Very good, thank you. Thank You.

Yeah. Call Mark Williamson win and my colleague, uh, and it's a real privilege to be here today to speak briefly to our submission, uh, on the bill. Uh, we'll be making, uh, four points. I was talking about four topics. I will do, uh, two, and then my colleague, uh, will, uh, do too as well.

Um, so I, I guess we'll get into it. Uh, my first topic, uh, which was touched on by the last submitter, is predatory pricing. And I guess, um, based on my, uh, experience of this era of working, uh, for, for the last 25 years, my view is that context and, uh, markets and firm structures matters, uh, with competition law.

And, uh, that's why, uh, I've always, um, valued and, and been supportive of New Zealand's, uh, principles, uh, based approach. Uh, which then gives the ability of the commission and the Corps to look at the individual facts, uh, look at at particular firms and, and reach a view on, uh, the application of the principle two markets, uh, to meet outcomes for the long-term benefit of consumers.

And, uh, my sense of, uh, the predatory pricing change is that, uh, the, the, the clause is, is really more in the nature of a guidance, uh, note, which, which is the, the European approach. And rather than seeking to, uh, prescriptively, you know, set out a test would be better to let the, uh, the, the, the, which effectively the new clause 36, uh, bid in and apply.

And the commission could, uh, again, adopt, uh, the guidance, uh, which, which again is guidance and it can be tailored, uh, by the commission and ultimately the court for the particular circumstance. Uh, so that, that's our, uh, submission on, uh, section 36, which is the predatory pricing.

And yeah, there's, there's more detail around that is set out in the submission around the definitions and the like, and how you apply those in complex, uh, multi-product markets, how you apply them to, to two-sided markets, uh, where there's a platform and, and, and in two markets.

It's, it's, these things are complex. Uh, that's not to say that, uh, there isn't a key role for the commerce act in those areas, but I think it's ultimately, uh, the best approach is to, to set the overall rule, which is we don't want a, uh, firm with substantial market power, uh, to substantially lessen competition.

And then how we get to that is best done, uh, through guidance in the court and, uh, and the like. So that's our submission on that. Uh, the next, uh, thing is the change to assets of a business. So, uh, I guess historically the was very clear in terms of how it approached, uh, structural regulation through, uh, part three and part two, which was more the, if you like, bear or contracts arrangements for understandings.

And there's, um, and, and the two, the two sections kind of worked in parallel. And if you think about it, there's, there's more scrutiny, uh, needed for contracts, arrangements for understandings, and, you know, bordering on cartel conduct because they don't have the degree of integration that you see in a, in, in, you know, if there is an acquisition of shares or a business, you know, coming together of parties to create efficiency.

So I, I guess, um, what I see in the change to assets is, is, is a blur or a, a movement towards, um, a, you know, bringing part three and part two close to together. And, and I guess, uh, when I looked at the Australian section, I saw that there is a carve out for, I think, business as usual transactions, because obviously there's a range of, um, assets that firms buy and sell in the ordinary course of business that you could imagine if there was a shortage of those, you know, it could actually have an impact on the market.

So that was my only, uh, submission on that point. It's just to look carefully at, um, any implications of making this change and, and moving the, the sort of blurring the lines between the two sections. And just to consider the, uh, position, uh, in Australia, which, um, as I said, has this kind of business as usual, uh, carve out.

So those are my two, uh, parts. Now I'll hand over to nu to speak to our other, uh, two points that we'd like to make. Thank you. Uh, good morning, and thank you for the opportunity to speak to our submission. I'll be talking about two topics. The first is, uh, behavioral undertakings, and the second is the proposed repeal of section 46 of the act.

Um, I'll talk first about behavioral undertakings. Um, as the committee will be aware, a lot of in has been spilled over the years on empowering the commission to accept behavioral undertakings in emerging context, I think was in 1989, the first time. Um, Andy suggested that, and it's not until today where it's actually been, uh, proposed to amend the, uh, act to empower the commission to accept them.

Uh, and we really do welcome that change, uh, for a couple of reasons. The first is that it aligns New Zealand's merger regime with Austral and other comparable international jurisdictions. Um, and second, it makes, uh, the act internally consistent. The commission is already able to accept behavior undertakings in other contexts.

And so ultimately this is a, it's another tool in the regulatory toolbox to get, uh, the efficient transfer of assets across the line. Um, that said, as it's currently pitched, we think that there are a couple of limits to how it's being proposed. Um, the first issue that we see is, um, whether or not it should be a backstop to a, uh, a divestment undertaking currently as it's proposed.

Um, the power to accept an undertaking will only be allowed where a divestment undertaking would be insufficient. Um, and, and leaving aside the issues with, um, the ambiguity around when a divestment is insufficient, um, we think that it should be open to parties to offer to the commission, um, and undertaking that is appropriate in their circumstances.

Um, and I guess the, the second issue that we see is currently section 93 or the proposed amendments to section 93 of the ACT inoculates the decision of the commission to accept the behavioral undertaking from any judicial scrutiny. Um, you know, we think that if parties of the view that, um, the commission should have accepted a behavioral undertaking, it should be open to them to test that proposition in a court.

Um, again, that's somewhat inconsistent with the rest of the act. Um, and I'll move now to the second, uh, aspect of my, uh, submission, which is the proposed repeal of section 48, uh, sorry, 46. Um, the effect of section 46 currently is that it inoculates merger, uh, sort of decisions or, um, from part two of the act, uh, restrictive trade practices, A mean or repealing Section 46 has the effect of testing those, uh, decisions against part two

of the act, uh, restrictive trade practices. Um, what that means is that for the first time section or clearance decisions are tested for and anti-competitive purpose, and it also means that they're no longer scrutinized just under a merger lens.

So some of the effects that, that we see are things like, um, I guess board papers and things that might suggest an anti-competitive purpose suddenly become, um, they become scrutinized by the commission for an anti-competitive purpose and other internal documents.

Um, so I guess ultimately, um, currently every acquisition removes, uh, well, is a contract arrangement understanding that remove, that could remove a competitor from the market. And so section 46 gives parties the confidence that their transaction will be tested under just the, uh, the merger lens.

And, uh, that takes us to the end of our submission, End, our submission. And we thank you, obviously, and you have heard this from a number of parties. We think the repeal of section 46, you know, it's, it's good to test, it's always good to check whether, um, you know, in an older piece of legislation, uh, every clause is still needed or every section.

But this one does have some, um, utility and it creates these unintended consequences, which I don't think were in the contemplation of, of MB or anyone when they, uh, put this forward. Thank you so much, uh, for your submission, your time. It really well. We don't have any time left for Christians.
Thank you. Have a good day. Bye-bye. Excellent. Thank you. Bye-bye. So the next we have the banking reform, uh, coalition. Thank you,

Uh, Kia. Good Morning. Is that Ken? Dustin, thank you so much. We have 10 minutes with you and you've seen how it works. Thank you. Thank you, Madam Chair. Uh, thank you members. Um, let me tell you a story about the price of bread, uh, because this goes to the heart of this particular bill back in the second half of last year, famously, the price of bread increased by 58%.

Uh, now this caused, uh, quite a bit of media coverage and concern. Um, the thing is, bread is actually pretty simple stuff. Uh, flour, water, salt, um, you know, some starter, uh, labor energy, you know, those kind of things. So the question is, why did the power, why did the price of bread go fif go up 58% when the price of flour only rose 5% and the price of labor weed up 2% and the price of electricity went up 12%?

Why did it go up 58%? And the answer is, well, it appears that we don't have competition in this country. We have a supermarket duopoly, uh, and we have oligopolies in practically every sector of the economy. So as a result, there is no good explanation as to why the price of bread went up 58% other than it could.

Now, this goes to the heart of the problems with this bill. Um, as a result of the, um, the Commerce Commission, Jo, uh, Dr. Small and his team can do nothing about the fact that the price of bread rose 58% when there appears to be no underlying competitive justification for that. Once this bill goes through, if this bill becomes law, the commission will still have no powers at all to do anything about the price of bread rising by 58%.

This bill is entirely ineffective. This bill will not achieve any of the, the objectives that the country requires, which is the return to competitive markets. So this is a structural failing with the bill, and it's a considerable concern to the banking reform coalition that the minister has put up a bill and wasted the time of parliament, of submitters, um, you know, of the, the country itself in making what are very small scale incremental changes that sort of just fiddle in the margins.

And the problem is that there is, this bill is predicated on the idea that the Commerce Act is working perfectly well and that, uh, it all needs is, all it needs is some minor tweaks in order to optimize it. And that's clearly not the case, because if that was the case, the price of bread would not have increased 58% when there was no underlying rationale for it.

Most of New Zealand's markets are dominated by oligopolies. That's the case in banking. It's the case in supermarkets, it's the case in building products. It's the case in electricity, and it's a very, very long list as the OECD tells us. And these, uh, oligopolies are extracting well above market returns for their activities.

So the banking, the profits outta the banking sector now constitute more than 3% of our entire GDP. So that's about three times larger than the equivalent in Europe. The return on, uh, uh, return on equity for a SB bank is at 22%. The OECD benchmark is 7%.

Nothing in this bill will address that. The, the issue with the, uh, with our competition law is that we require the restructuring of markets. So the, the, the, the fundamental premise of this bill is that markets are working efficiently when they are not. In order to be able to get the restructuring of markets, the commission needs to be empowered to step in and restructure them.

Exactly has happened with Telecom New Zealand back in the 2010s where we said we have a, an oppressive, uh, a monopoly in that case that was holding the country back and the country that the parliament stepped in legislated for this and broke it up. And we can see the positive results that have come from this since.

Now. The problem right now is that at the moment, we require separate legislation for each of the dysfunctional markets that occurs. So we would require separate legislation for supermarkets, separate legislation for banks, separate legislation for electricity companies. This is clearly unworkable, given the scale of the damage that has been done to our economy by the pernicious effects of oligopolies.

So we are advocating that this bill is sent back and redrafted, and the purpose of the redraft is to adopt what is timidly presented as option three, uh, in the R, which is to say the commission is given the regulatory power to be able to restructure markets without the approval of cabinet.

Now, the important part is the approval of cabinet. And we've seen, uh, in this administration under Minister Simpson decisions coming outta cabinet or indecisions coming out of cabinet that are clearly not in the best interest of New Zealanders. And the one I'm gonna draw attention to is the triple CFA amendment.

Now this was a technical, uh, change and it had some useful things in it, but as a result of the lobbying that clearly occurred with Minister Simpson, it also removed. Well, you know, uh, if the bill had proceeded as Minister Simpson proposed, it would've stopped, uh, a court case in flight.

That was a class action to get back from, uh, the Australian owned banks excess fees that they had charged. Mm-hmm. Minister Simpson's actions would have stopped the settlement that just occurred with a SB bank prior to Christmas that returned $136 million from the Australian owned banks back to New Zealanders.

So we have a Minister of Commerce who put forward an amendment to make Australians richer at the cost of New Zealanders. Now, when we are seeing these kinds of decisions out of cabinet, when we are seeing, for instance, that as is well known and reported in the media Minister Simpson's families, family owned supermarkets, we are not seeing any reform of the supermarket sector exactly.

Nothing has occurred to bring down prices and supermarkets during Minister Simpson's reign. Uh, bank profits have risen to historical highs. Supermarket margins have risen to historical highs. Electricity profits have risen to historical highs. So whoever Minister Simpson's working for it clearly doesn't appear to be us.

So as a result of this, can I just say these amendments? Can I just, uh, say that, that look ministers, usually it's a very high threshold because people say lots of things about ministers, but I just want to caution you about where you're going with this. Doesn't have the, uh, thank, thank you Madam Chair. Uh, I point out that nothing I've said is factually inaccurate.

Um, the important thing here in this context is difficult to trust ministerial judgment and cabinet judgment when we see the perpetuation of these oligopolies. Therefore, we are recommending that this bill be sent back for redrafting with the effect that it is the regulator that can restructure markets and not the politicians of the day.

Because this insulates the country from the effects of lobbying that can occur during a political process, when in fact, the restructuring of a market is a thing that is entirely obvious as needs to happen in, uh, some fundamental areas of our economy. And no progress is being made on this from the political side.

Mm-hmm. And also, I just want you to, uh, be aware that Minister Simpson doesn't have delegation for supermarkets Exactly. For the reason he declared the conflict of interest, as far as I remember. Uh, yes. And you'll be aware, of course, that, uh, in the media recently, uh, uh, as a result of the RFI that was put out by the Minister of Finance, uh, there were no respondents at all.

No meetings have been held and supermarket prices and the price of bread went up by 58%, uh, prior to Christmas. There was any questions, uh, Dr. David, who's assembling the evidence, Uh, the Commerce Commission itself, uh, which will require, of course, it does have all of the rigors of the market studies.

Uh, and having participated in a couple of the market studies, I'd have to call out the quality and excellence of the work being done by Dr. Small and his team. Uh, the quality of the market studies is exemplary by international standards. Thank you, Kim. Any other questions or clarifications? Members? Uh, thank you for your time.
Thank you for your submission. Thank You. Uh, thanks. We have Patrick, do we now in the room here, Patrick? Yes, please come forward.
I might just Yeah, Of course. Yeah. Hold this page.

So when you're ready, Patrick, we have five minutes with you. Yes, thank you. And after you finish, uh, speaking to your submission, there might be questions from him. Thank you. Thank you very much. Excuse me. Thank you, chair, for the opportunity to make this, uh, oral submission. It's based on my experience, particularly as general manager finance for, excuse me, telecom Sparks predecessor, and as my two terms as commissioner at the Commerce Commission, I'm currently a finance and economic expert lay member of the high court

under the Commerce Act. And that has, uh, limited some of the comments I would make because of matters that might come, I might be involved in a different capacity. Um, I would like you to please put up the first slide if you could. Can you do that

While that's happening? Um, in enacting the bill, parliament will strengthen the commission's power to promote competition in New Zealand markets. As always, an economic policy achieving that benefit will incur costs including inhibiting achievement of economies of scale and inflicting compliance costs.

The side that you have, uh, illustrates that issue, the words are mine, but the images from Google Gemini, um, it's a wonderful thing. AI in terms of making presentations look more attractive. I believe the benefits will outweigh the costs because the commission's exercise of its new powers will be governed under the reformed structure of the Commerce Commission, uh, in the reform bill that is being referred to this committee last week.

And my comments, therefore are covering in a sense or in the context that that bill will change the, uh, way the governance works. Um, the slide that, uh, the second of my slides, um, which you have, I hope yes, we have a copy, illustrates my concept of how the new governance structure should work to achieve the promotion of competition, while minimizing the cost to the, the concept is of an air traffic controller, uh, who is controlling the, um, aircraft.

And in a way, the board, um, the new governance board will be in the position, uh, similar to that in as much as it doesn't make the regulatory decisions, but does guide the way they're made. It will be challenging to make that structure work entirely smoothly.

Um, but I'll comment on that and a submission I intend to make on that bill. So I'll leave there. Um, the submissions to this committee have included concerns regarding the proposed new powers, including the authority to initiate market studies and to compel provision of information and analysis.

I, I believe myself that these new powers are warranted. As a commissioner, I did experience the resistance of market, some market participants, uh, not a minority regarding the commission undertaking analysis of suppliers behavior, and of market structure.

Nevertheless, the new powers are potentially quite intrusive. The governance reform in effect separates overall governance from the specific exercise of those powers that should help ensure the powers are appropriately applied. In my written submission, I suggest this committee recommend to parliament, excuse me, I'm sorry,

recommend to parliament insertion of requirements that the commission published documents on the frameworks under which it will exercise the new powers it will have under the bill's provisions. Such requirements would need to be tailored to each of the specific new powers, and the committee would need officials to advise on the suitable statement of requirements.

As a commissioner, I was, um, uh, heavily involved in the implementation of the, what was then the new part four of the Commerce Act. And we were required by that act or that, uh, modification to determine a set of input methodologies, which is an obscure term, but it was the framework which would govern the exercise of the specific regulatory powers, and in particular, determine components such as the cost of capital, specifying the methodology, not the outcome.

Obviously, a similar approach could be applied regarding aspects of the new powers. And on the slides I have, uh, illustrated, um, at the, you know, the particular way that could apply in my, um, written submission. I provide more detail to chair.

Uh, so sorry to interrupt, Patrick. It's already five minutes. Do you want I just have a final closing paragraph. Yeah. Um, if the commission was required to issue such frameworks as determinations, that, and, and that's the term i I wanted to mention, that would mean effective parties could apply to the courts for merits review of the frameworks to assist the committee.

I've asked the secretariat to provide you with copies of a recent high court judgment that explains the difference between merits reviews and judicial reviews. And also I might add is some interest in itself and I think you would find valuable in your consideration of Bill.

It does point out that if the commission makes a mistake, which is what that's about, it um, causes a lot of problems. I hope it wouldn't make that type of mistake. Again, Thank you so much for your submission. Thank you for your time. Ms. Um, members, we go into practice.
